name: pybtex

metadata.name: pybtex
metadata.summary: A BibTeX-compatible bibliography processor in Python

documentation:
# BibTeX-compatible bibliography processor in Python

## Synopsis

```
latex foo.tex
pybtex foo.aux
latex foo.tex
latex foo.tex
```

## Description

Pybtex reads citation information from a file and
produces a formatted bibliography. BibTeX style files are supported.
Alternatively it is possible to write styles in Python.

Pybtex currently understands the following bibliography formats:

- BibTeX
- BibTeXML
- YAML-based format

The resulting bibliography may be output in one of the following formats
(not supported by legacy BibTeX styles):

- LaTeX
- HTML
- markdown
- plain text

## See also

- [Home page](https://pybtex.org/)
- [Pybtex at Bitbucket](https://bitbucket.org/pybtex-devs/pybtex)
- [Pybtex at PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/pybtex)

modules.name: pybtex.database.input.bibtex
documentation:
BibTeX parser

```pycon
>>> parser = Parser()
>>> bib_data = parser.parse_string(u'''
... @String{SCI = "Science"}
...
... @String{JFernandez = "Fernandez, Julio M."}
... @String{HGaub = "Gaub, Hermann E."}
... @String{MGautel = "Gautel, Mathias"}
... @String{FOesterhelt = "Oesterhelt, Filipp"}
... @String{MRief = "Rief, Matthias"}
...
... @Article{rief97b,
...   author =       MRief #" and "# MGautel #" and "# FOesterhelt
...                  #" and "# JFernandez #" and "# HGaub,
...   title =        "Reversible Unfolding of Individual Titin
...                  Immunoglobulin Domains by {AFM}",
...   journal =      SCI,
...   volume =       276,
...   number =       5315,
...   pages =        "1109--1112",
...   year =         1997,
...   doi =          "10.1126/science.276.5315.1109",
...   URL =          "http://www.sciencemag.org/cgi/content/abstract/276/5315/1109",
...   eprint =       "http://www.sciencemag.org/cgi/reprint/276/5315/1109.pdf",
... }
... ''')
```

\# entry keys are case-insensitive
\>>> bib_data.entries\['rief97b'\] == bib_data.entries\['RIEF97B'\]
True

```pycon
>>> rief97b = bib_data.entries['rief97b']
>>> authors = rief97b.persons['author']
>>> for author in authors:
...     print(six.text_type(author))
Rief, Matthias
Gautel, Mathias
Oesterhelt, Filipp
Fernandez, Julio M.
Gaub, Hermann E.
```

\# field names are case-insensitive
\>>> print(rief97b.fields\['URL'\])
<http://www.sciencemag.org/cgi/content/abstract/276/5315/1109>
\>>> print(rief97b.fields\['url'\])
<http://www.sciencemag.org/cgi/content/abstract/276/5315/1109>

classes.name: pybtex.database.output.bibtex.Writer

methods.name: pybtex.database.output.bibtex.Writer.quote
documentation:
```pycon
>>> w = Writer()
>>> print(w.quote('The World'))
"The World"
>>> print(w.quote(r'The \emph{World}'))
"The \emph{World}"
>>> print(w.quote(r'The "World"'))
{The "World"}
>>> try:
...     print(w.quote(r'The {World'))
... except BibTeXError as error:
...     print(error)
String has unmatched braces: The {World
```

methods.name: pybtex.database.output.bibtex.Writer.check_braces
documentation:
Raise an exception if the given string has unmatched braces.

```pycon
>>> w = Writer()
>>> w.check_braces('Cat eats carrots.')
>>> w.check_braces('Cat eats {carrots}.')
>>> w.check_braces('Cat eats {carrots{}}.')
>>> w.check_braces('')
>>> w.check_braces('end}')
>>> try:
...     w.check_braces('{')
... except BibTeXError as error:
...     print(error)
String has unmatched braces: {
>>> w.check_braces('{test}}')
>>> try:
...     w.check_braces('{{test}')
... except BibTeXError as error:
...     print(error)
String has unmatched braces: {{test}
```

methods.name: pybtex.database.output.bibtex.Writer._encode
documentation:
Encode text as LaTeX.

```pycon
>>> w = Writer(encoding='ASCII')
>>> print(w._encode(u'1970–1971.'))
1970--1971.
```

```pycon
>>> w = Writer(encoding='UTF-8')
>>> print(w._encode(u'1970–1971.'))
1970–1971.
```

```pycon
>>> w = Writer(encoding='UTF-8')
>>> print(w._encode(u'100% noir'))
100\% noir
```

methods.name: pybtex.database.output.bibtex.Writer._encode_with_comments
documentation:
Encode text as LaTeX, preserve comments.

```pycon
>>> w = Writer(encoding='ASCII')
>>> print(w._encode_with_comments(u'1970–1971.  %% † RIP †'))
1970--1971.  %% \dag\ RIP \dag
```

```pycon
>>> w = Writer(encoding='UTF-8')
>>> print(w._encode_with_comments(u'1970–1971.  %% † RIP †'))
1970–1971.  %% † RIP †
```
Outputs BibTeX markup

classes.name: pybtex.utils.CaseInsensitiveDict

methods.name: pybtex.utils.CaseInsensitiveDict.__setitem__
documentation: To implement lowercase keys.

methods.name: pybtex.utils.CaseInsensitiveDict.__repr__
documentation: A caselessDict version of __repr__

A dict with case-insensitive lookup.

```pycon
>>> d = CaseInsensitiveDict(TesT='passed')
>>> d
CaseInsensitiveDict({'TesT': u'passed'})
>>> d.lower()
CaseInsensitiveDict({'test': u'passed'})
>>> print(d['TesT'])
passed
>>> print(d['test'])
passed
>>> print(d['Test'])
passed
>>> print(d.get('test'))
passed
>>> print(d.get('Test'))
passed
```

```pycon
>>> d['Test'] = 'passed again'
>>> print(d['test'])
passed again
>>> 'test' in d
True
>>> 'Test' in d
True
>>> list(d.keys())
[u'Test']
>>> for key in d:
...     print(key)
Test
>>> for key, value in d.items():
...     print(key, value)
Test passed again
>>> bool(d)
True
>>> len(d)
1
```

```pycon
>>> del d['test']
>>> len(d)
0
>>> bool(d)
False
>>> 'test' in d
False
>>> 'Test' in d
False
>>> print(d.get('test'))
None
>>> print(d.get('Test'))
None
>>> print(d.get('Test', 'failed'))
failed
```

```pycon
>>> CaseInsensitiveDict(
...     (key, value) for key, value in [('a', 'b')]
... )
CaseInsensitiveDict({u'a': u'b'})
```

classes.name: pybtex.utils.CaseInsensitiveDefaultDict
documentation:
CaseInseisitiveDict with default factory, like collections.defaultdict

```pycon
>>> d = CaseInsensitiveDefaultDict(int)
>>> d['a']
0
>>> d['a'] += 1
>>> d['a']
1
>>> d['A']
1
>>> d['a'] = 3
>>> d['a']
3
>>> d['B'] += 10
>>> d['b']
10
```

classes.name: pybtex.utils.OrderedCaseInsensitiveDict
documentation:
An (incomplete) ordered case-insensitive dict.

```pycon
>>> d = OrderedCaseInsensitiveDict([
...     ('Uno', 1),
...     ('Dos', 2),
...     ('Tres', 3),
... ])
>>> d
OrderedCaseInsensitiveDict([(u'Uno', 1), (u'Dos', 2), (u'Tres', 3)])
>>> d.lower()
OrderedCaseInsensitiveDict([(u'uno', 1), (u'dos', 2), (u'tres', 3)])
>>> list(d.keys())
[u'Uno', u'Dos', u'Tres']
>>> list(d.items())
[(u'Uno', 1), (u'Dos', 2), (u'Tres', 3)]
>>> list(d.values())
[1, 2, 3]
>>> d['Cuatro'] = 4
>>> list(d.keys())
[u'Uno', u'Dos', u'Tres', u'Cuatro']
>>> list(d.items())
[(u'Uno', 1), (u'Dos', 2), (u'Tres', 3), (u'Cuatro', 4)]
>>> list(d.values())
[1, 2, 3, 4]
>>> list(d)
[u'Uno', u'Dos', u'Tres', u'Cuatro']
>>> 'Uno' in d
True
>>> 'uno' in d
True
>>> d['Uno']
1
>>> d['uno']
1
>>> d['UNO']
1
>>> 'Cuatro' in d
True
>>> 'CUATRO' in d
True
>>> d['Cuatro']
4
>>> d['cuatro']
4
>>> d['UNO'] = 'one'
>>> d['uno']
u'one'
>>> d['Uno']
u'one'
>>> list(d.keys())
[u'UNO', u'Dos', u'Tres', u'Cuatro']
>>> d['cuatro'] = 'four'
>>> d['Cuatro']
u'four'
>>> d['cuatro']
u'four'
>>> list(d.keys())
[u'UNO', u'Dos', u'Tres', u'cuatro']
>>> list(d.values())
[u'one', 2, 3, u'four']
>>> del d['dos']
>>> list(d.keys())
[u'UNO', u'Tres', u'cuatro']
>>> list(d.values())
[u'one', 3, u'four']
```

classes.name: pybtex.utils.CaseInsensitiveSet

methods.name: pybtex.utils.CaseInsensitiveSet.__repr__
documentation: A caselessDict version of __repr__

A very basic case-insensitive set.

```pycon
>>> s = CaseInsensitiveSet()
>>> len(s)
0
>>> 'a' in s
False
>>> list(CaseInsensitiveSet(['aaa', 'Aaa', 'AAA']))
[u'aaa']
>>> s = CaseInsensitiveSet(['Aaa', 'Bbb'])
>>> s
CaseInsensitiveSet([u'Aaa', u'Bbb'])
>>> s.lower()
CaseInsensitiveSet([u'aaa', u'bbb'])
>>> len(s)
2
>>> 'aaa' in s
True
>>> 'Aaa' in s
True
>>> 'AAA' in s
True
>>> 'bbb' in s
True
>>> 'Bbb' in s
True
>>> 'abc' in s
False
>>> s.add('ccc')
>>> len(s)
3
>>> 'aaa' in s
True
>>> 'ccc' in s
True
>>> s.remove('AAA')
>>> len(s)
2
>>> 'aaa' in s
False
```

```pycon
>>> bool(CaseInsensitiveSet(['a']))
True
>>> bool(CaseInsensitiveSet([]))
False
>>> bool(CaseInsensitiveSet())
False
```

functions.name: pybtex.plugin.find_plugin
documentation:
Find a {class}`Plugin` class within *plugin_group* which
matches *name*, or *filename* if *name* is not specified, or
the default plugin if neither *name* nor *filename* is
specified.

If *name* is specified, return the {class}`Plugin` class
registered under *name*. If *filename* is specified, look at
its suffix (i.e. extension) and return the {class}`Plugin`
class registered for this suffix.

If *name* is not a string, but a plugin class, just return it back.
(Used to make functions like {function}`make_bibliography` accept already
loaded plugins as well as plugin names.

functions.name: pybtex.plugin.enumerate_plugin_names
documentation: Enumerate all plugin names for the given *plugin_group*.

functions.name: pybtex.plugin.register_plugin
documentation:
Register a plugin on the fly.

This works by adding *klass* as a pybtex entry point, under entry
point group *plugin_group* and entry point name *name*.

To register a suffix, append ".suffixes" to the plugin group, and
*name* is then simply the suffix, which should start with a
period.

To register an alias plugin name, append ".aliases" to the plugin group.
Aliases work just as real names, but are not returned by
{function}`enumerate_plugin_names` and not advertised in the command line
help.

If *force* is `False`, then existing entry points are not
overwritten. If an entry point with the given group and name
already exists, then returns `False`, otherwise returns
`True`.

If *force* is `True`, then existing entry points are
overwritten, and the function always returns `True`.

methods.name: pybtex.database.BibliographyData.__init__
documentation:
A {py:class}`.BibliographyData` object contains a dictionary of bibliography
entries referenced by their keys.
Each entry represented by an {py:class}`.Entry` object.

Additionally, {py:class}`.BibliographyData` may contain a LaTeX
preamble defined by `@PREAMBLE` commands in the BibTeX file.

methods.name: pybtex.database.BibliographyData.preamble
documentation:
LaTeX preamble.

```pycon
>>> bib_data = parse_string(r"""
...     @PREAMBLE{"\newcommand{\noopsort}[1]{}"}
... """, 'bibtex')
>>> print(bib_data.preamble)
\newcommand{\noopsort}[1]{}
```

:::{versionadded} 0.19 Earlier versions used :py:meth:`.get_preamble()`, which is now deprecated.
:::

methods.name: pybtex.database.BibliographyData.get_preamble
documentation:
:::{deprecated} 0.19 Use :py:attr:`.preamble` instead.
:::

methods.name: pybtex.database.BibliographyData._get_crossreferenced_citations
documentation:
Get cititations not cited explicitly but referenced by other citations.

```pycon
>>> from pybtex.database import Entry
>>> data = BibliographyData({
...     'main_article': Entry('article', {'crossref': 'xrefd_arcicle'}),
...     'xrefd_arcicle': Entry('article'),
... })
>>> list(data._get_crossreferenced_citations([], min_crossrefs=1))
[]
>>> list(data._get_crossreferenced_citations(['main_article'], min_crossrefs=1))
[u'xrefd_arcicle']
>>> list(data._get_crossreferenced_citations(['Main_article'], min_crossrefs=1))
[u'xrefd_arcicle']
>>> list(data._get_crossreferenced_citations(['main_article'], min_crossrefs=2))
[]
>>> list(data._get_crossreferenced_citations(['xrefd_arcicle'], min_crossrefs=1))
[]
```

```pycon
>>> data2 = BibliographyData(data.entries, wanted_entries=data.entries.keys())
>>> list(data2._get_crossreferenced_citations([], min_crossrefs=1))
[]
>>> list(data2._get_crossreferenced_citations(['main_article'], min_crossrefs=1))
[u'xrefd_arcicle']
>>> list(data2._get_crossreferenced_citations(['Main_article'], min_crossrefs=1))
[u'xrefd_arcicle']
>>> list(data2._get_crossreferenced_citations(['main_article'], min_crossrefs=2))
[]
>>> list(data2._get_crossreferenced_citations(['xrefd_arcicle'], min_crossrefs=1))
[]
>>> list(data2._get_crossreferenced_citations(['xrefd_arcicle'], min_crossrefs=1))
[]
```

methods.name: pybtex.database.BibliographyData._expand_wildcard_citations
documentation:
Expand wildcard citations (citation{\*} in .aux file).

```pycon
>>> from pybtex.database import Entry
>>> data = BibliographyData((
...     ('uno', Entry('article')),
...     ('dos', Entry('article')),
...     ('tres', Entry('article')),
...     ('cuatro', Entry('article')),
... ))
>>> list(data._expand_wildcard_citations([]))
[]
>>> list(data._expand_wildcard_citations(['*']))
[u'uno', u'dos', u'tres', u'cuatro']
>>> list(data._expand_wildcard_citations(['uno', '*']))
[u'uno', u'dos', u'tres', u'cuatro']
>>> list(data._expand_wildcard_citations(['dos', '*']))
[u'dos', u'uno', u'tres', u'cuatro']
>>> list(data._expand_wildcard_citations(['*', 'uno']))
[u'uno', u'dos', u'tres', u'cuatro']
>>> list(data._expand_wildcard_citations(['*', 'DOS']))
[u'uno', u'dos', u'tres', u'cuatro']
```

methods.name: pybtex.database.BibliographyData.lower
documentation:
Return another {py:class}`.BibliographyData` with all identifiers converted to lowercase.

```pycon
>>> data = parse_string("""
...     @BOOK{Obrazy,
...         title = "Obrazy z Rus",
...         author = "Karel Havlíček Borovský",
...     }
...     @BOOK{Elegie,
...         title = "Tirolské elegie",
...         author = "Karel Havlíček Borovský",
...     }
... """, 'bibtex')
>>> data_lower = data.lower()
>>> list(data_lower.entries.keys())
[u'obrazy', u'elegie']
>>> for entry in data_lower.entries.values():
...     entry.key
...     list(entry.persons.keys())
...     list(entry.fields.keys())
u'obrazy'
[u'author']
[u'title']
u'elegie'
[u'author']
[u'title']
```

methods.name: pybtex.database.Entry._find_field
documentation:
Find the field with the given `name` according to this rules:

- If the given field `name` in in `self.fields`, just return
  self.fields\[name\].
- Otherwise, if `name` is `"authors"` or `"editors"` (or any other
  person role), return the list of names as a string, separated by
  `" and "`.
- Otherwise, if this entry has a `crossreff` field, look up for the
  cross-referenced entry and try to find its field with the given
  `name`.

methods.name: pybtex.database.Person.__init__
documentation:
---
param string: |-
  The full name string.
  It will be parsed and split into separate first, last, middle,
  pre-last and lineage name parst.

  Supported name formats are:

  - von Last, First
  - von Last, Jr, First
  - First von Last

  (see BibTeX manual for explanation)
---

methods.name: pybtex.database.Person.bibtex_first_names
documentation:
A list of first and middle names together.
(BibTeX treats all middle names as first.)

:::{versionadded} 0.19 Earlier versions used :py:meth:`Person.bibtex_first`, which is now deprecated.
:::

```pycon
>>> knuth = Person('Donald E. Knuth')
>>> knuth.bibtex_first_names
[u'Donald', u'E.']
```

methods.name: pybtex.database.Person._parse_string
documentation:
Extract various parts of the name from a string.

```pycon
>>> p = Person('Avinash K. Dixit')
>>> print(p.first_names)
[u'Avinash']
>>> print(p.middle_names)
[u'K.']
>>> print(p.prelast_names)
[]
>>> print(p.last_names)
[u'Dixit']
>>> print(p.lineage_names)
[]
>>> print(six.text_type(p))
Dixit, Avinash K.
>>> p == Person(six.text_type(p))
True
>>> p = Person('Dixit, Jr, Avinash K. ')
>>> print(p.first_names)
[u'Avinash']
>>> print(p.middle_names)
[u'K.']
>>> print(p.prelast_names)
[]
>>> print(p.last_names)
[u'Dixit']
>>> print(p.lineage_names)
[u'Jr']
>>> print(six.text_type(p))
Dixit, Jr, Avinash K.
>>> p == Person(six.text_type(p))
True
```

```pycon
>>> p = Person('abc')
>>> print(p.first_names, p.middle_names, p.prelast_names, p.last_names, p.lineage_names)
[] [] [] [u'abc'] []
>>> p = Person('Viktorov, Michail~Markovitch')
>>> print(p.first_names, p.middle_names, p.prelast_names, p.last_names, p.lineage_names)
[u'Michail'] [u'Markovitch'] [] [u'Viktorov'] []
```

documentation: : the result of render and render_sequence

class_variables.name: pybtex.backends.BaseBackend.default_suffix
documentation: : the default suffix for an output file

This is the base class for the backends. We encourage
you to implement as many of the symbols and tags as
possible when you create a new plugin.

symbols[u'ndash']    : Used to separate pages
symbols[u'newblock'] : Used to separate entries in the bibliography
symbols[u'nbsp']     : A non-breakable space

tags[u'em']          : emphasize text
tags[u'strong']      : emphasize text even more
tags[u'i']           : italicize text, not semantic
tags[u'b']           : embolden text, not semantic
tags[u'tt']          : typewrite text, not semantic

modules.name: pybtex.backends.markdown
documentation:
Markdown output backend.

```pycon
>>> from pybtex.richtext import Tag, HRef
>>> markdown = Backend()
>>> print(Tag('em', '').render(markdown))
<BLANKLINE>
>>> print(Tag('em', 'Non-', 'empty').render(markdown))
*Non\-empty*

<sup>superman</sup>
>>> print(HRef('/', '').render(markdown))
<BLANKLINE>
>>> print(HRef('/', 'Non-', 'empty').render(markdown))
[Non\-empty](/)
```

Escapes special markdown control characters.

A backend to support markdown output. It implements the same
features as the HTML backend.

In addition to that, you can use the keyword php_extra=True to enable
the definition list extension of php-markdown. The default is not to use
it, since we cannot be sure that this feature is implemented on all
systems.

More information:
http://www.michelf.com/projects/php-markdown/extra/#def-list

methods.name: pybtex.PybtexEngine.format_from_files
documentation:
Read the bigliography data from the given files and produce a formated
bibliography.

```
param bib_files_or_filenames

A list of file names or file objects.

param style

The name of the formatting style.

param citations

A list of citation keys.

param bib_format

The name of the bibliography format. The default
format is bibtex.

param bib_encoding

Encoding of bibliography files.

param output_backend

Which output backend to use. The default is latex.

param output_encoding

Encoding that will be used by the output backend.

param bst_encoding

Encoding of the .bst file.

param min_crossrefs

Include cross-referenced entries after this many
crossrefs. See BibTeX manual for details.

param output_filename

If None, the result will be returned as a
string. Else, the result will be written to the specified file.

param add_output_suffix

Append default suffix to the output file
name (.bbl for LaTeX, .html for HTML, etc.).
```

The Python fomatting engine.

See {py:class}`pybtex.Engine` for inherited methods.

methods.name: pybtex.style.names.lastfirst.NameStyle.format
documentation: Format names similarly to {vv~}\{ll}{, jj}{, f.} in BibTeX. ```pycon >>> from pybtex.database import Person >>> name = Person(string=r"Charles Louis Xavier Joseph de la Vall{\'e}e Poussin") >>> lastfirst = NameStyle().format ``` ```pycon >>> print(lastfirst(name).format().render_as('latex')) de~la Vall{é}e~Poussin, Charles Louis Xavier~Joseph >>> print(lastfirst(name).format().render_as('html')) de la Vallée Poussin, Charles Louis Xavier Joseph ``` ```pycon >>> print(lastfirst(name, abbr=True).format().render_as('latex')) de~la Vall{é}e~Poussin, C.~L. X.~J. >>> print(lastfirst(name, abbr=True).format().render_as('html')) de la Vallée Poussin, C. L. X. J. ``` ```pycon >>> name = Person(first='First', last='Last', middle='Middle') >>> print(lastfirst(name).format().render_as('latex')) Last, First~Middle >>> print(lastfirst(name, abbr=True).format().render_as('latex')) Last, F.~M. ```

modules.name: pybtex.style.template
documentation:
A template engine for bibliography entries and more.

Inspired by Brevé -- <http://breve.twisty-industries.com/>

```pycon
>>> from pybtex.database import Entry, Person
>>> author = Person(first='First', last='Last', middle='Middle')
>>> fields = {
...         'title': 'The Book',
...         'year': '2000',
... }
>>> e = Entry('book', fields=fields)
>>> book_format = sentence(capfirst=True, sep=', ') [
...     field('title'), field('year'), optional [field('sdf')]
... ]
>>> print(six.text_type(book_format.format_data({'entry': e})))
The Book, 2000.
>>> print(six.text_type(words ['one', 'two', words ['three', 'four']].format_data(e)))
one two three four
```

functions.name: pybtex.style.template.join
documentation:
Join text fragments together.
\>>> print(six.text_type(join.format()))
\<BLANKLINE>
\>>> print(six.text_type(join \['a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e'\].format()))
abcde
\>>> print(six.text_type(join(sep=', ', sep2=' and ', last_sep=', and ') \['Tom', 'Jerry'\].format()))
Tom and Jerry
\>>> print(six.text_type(join(sep=', ', sep2=' and ', last_sep=', and ') \['Billy', 'Willy', 'Dilly'\].format()))
Billy, Willy, and Dilly

functions.name: pybtex.style.template.words
documentation: Join text fragments with spaces or something else.

functions.name: pybtex.style.template.together
documentation:
Try to keep words together, like BibTeX does.

```pycon
>>> print(six.text_type(together ['very', 'long', 'road'].format()))
very long road
>>> print(six.text_type(together(last_tie=True) ['very', 'long', 'road'].format()))
very long<nbsp>road
>>> print(six.text_type(together ['a', 'very', 'long', 'road'].format()))
a<nbsp>very long road
>>> print(six.text_type(together ['chapter', '8'].format()))
chapter<nbsp>8
>>> print(six.text_type(together ['chapter', '666'].format()))
chapter 666
```

functions.name: pybtex.style.template.sentence
documentation:
Join text fragments, capitalyze the first letter, add a period to the end.

```pycon
>>> print(six.text_type(sentence.format()))
<BLANKLINE>
>>> print(six.text_type(sentence(capitalize=True, sep=' ') ['mary', 'had', 'a', 'little', 'lamb'].format()))
Mary had a little lamb.
>>> print(six.text_type(sentence(capitalize=False, add_period=False) ['uno', 'dos', 'tres'].format()))
uno, dos, tres
```

functions.name: pybtex.style.template.optional
documentation:
If children contain a missing bibliography field, return None.
Else return formatted children.

```pycon
>>> from pybtex.database import Entry
>>> template = optional [field('volume'), optional['(', field('number'), ')']]
>>> template.format_data({'entry': Entry('article')})
Text()
```

```pycon
>>> join(', ')
join(u', ')
>>> join
join
>>> join ['a']
join [u'a']
>>> join ['a', 'b', 'c']
join [u'a', u'b', u'c']
>>> join(' ') [u'a', u'b', u'c']
join(u' ') [u'a', u'b', u'c']
>>> join(sep=' ') [u'a', u'b', u'c']
join(sep=u' ') [u'a', u'b', u'c']
>>> join(sep=u' ') [tag('em') [u'a', u'b', u'c']]
```

functions.name: pybtex.textutils.is_terminated
documentation:
Return True if text ends with a terminating character.

```pycon
>>> is_terminated('')
False
>>> is_terminated('.')
True
>>> is_terminated('Done')
False
>>> is_terminated('Done. ')
False
>>> is_terminated('Done.')
True
>>> is_terminated('Done...')
True
>>> is_terminated('Done!')
True
>>> is_terminated('Done?')
True
>>> is_terminated('Done?!')
True
```

functions.name: pybtex.textutils.add_period
documentation:
Add a period to the end of text, if needed.

```pycon
>>> print(add_period(''))
<BLANKLINE>
>>> print(add_period('.'))
.
>>> print(add_period('Done'))
Done.
>>> print(add_period('Done. '))
Done. .
>>> print(add_period('Done.'))
Done.
>>> print(add_period('Done...'))
Done...
>>> print(add_period('Done!'))
Done!
>>> print(add_period('Done?'))
Done?
>>> print(add_period('Done?!'))
Done?!
```

functions.name: pybtex.textutils.abbreviate
documentation:
Abbreviate the given text.

\>> abbreviate('Name')
u'N'
\>> abbreviate('Some words')
u'S. w.'
\>>> abbreviate('First-Second')
u'F.-S.'

functions.name: pybtex.textutils.normalize_whitespace
documentation:
Replace every sequence of whitespace characters with a single space.

```pycon
>>> print(normalize_whitespace('abc'))
abc
>>> print(normalize_whitespace('Abc def.'))
Abc def.
>>> print(normalize_whitespace(' Abc def.'))
Abc def.
>>> print(normalize_whitespace('Abc\ndef.'))
Abc def.
>>> print(normalize_whitespace('Abc\r\ndef.'))
Abc def.
>>> print(normalize_whitespace('Abc    \r\n\tdef.'))
Abc def.
>>> print(normalize_whitespace('   \nAbc\r\ndef.'))
Abc def.
```

functions.name: pybtex.textutils.width
documentation:
Get the width of the typeset string, in relative units.  Similar to
BibTeX's width\$, but does not care about any "special characters".

```pycon
>>> width('')
0
>>> width('abc')
1500
>>> width('ab{c}')
2500
>>> width(r"ab{\'c}")
3278
>>> width(r"ab{\'c{}}")
4278
>>> width(r"ab{\'c{}")
3778
>>> width(r"ab{\'c{d}}")
4834
```

methods.name: pybtex.bibtex.BibTeXEngine.format_from_files
documentation:
Read the bigliography data from the given files and produce a formated
bibliography.

```
param bib_files_or_filenames

A list of file names or file objects.

param style

The name of the formatting style.

param citations

A list of citation keys.

param bib_format

The name of the bibliography format. The default
format is bibtex.

param bib_encoding

Encoding of bibliography files.

param output_encoding

Encoding that will be used by the output backend.

param bst_encoding

Encoding of the .bst file.

param min_crossrefs

Include cross-referenced entries after this many
crossrefs. See BibTeX manual for details.

param output_filename

If None, the result will be returned as a
string. Else, the result will be written to the specified file.

param add_output_suffix

Append a .bbl suffix to the output file name.
```

The Python fomatting engine.

See {py:class}`pybtex.Engine` for inherited methods.

modules.name: pybtex.bibtex.names
documentation:
BibTeX-like name formatting.

```pycon
>>> name = 'Charles Louis Xavier Joseph de la Vallee Poussin'
>>> print(format_name(name, '{vv~}{ll}{, jj}{, f.}'))
de~la Vallee~Poussin, C.~L. X.~J.
>>> name = 'abc'
>>> print(format_name(name, '{vv~}{ll}{, jj}{, f.}'))
abc
>>> name = 'Jean-Pierre Hansen'
>>> print(format_name(name, '{ff~}{vv~}{ll}{, jj}'))
Jean-Pierre Hansen
>>> print(format_name(name, '{f.~}{vv~}{ll}{, jj}'))
J.-P. Hansen
```

```pycon
>>> name = 'F. Phidias Phony-Baloney'
>>> print(format_name(name, '{v{}}{l}'))
P.-B
>>> print(format_name(name, '{v{}}{l.}'))
P.-B.
>>> print(format_name(name, '{v{}}{l{}}'))
PB
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.names.join
documentation:
Join some words, inserting ties (~) when nessessary.
Ties are inserted:
\- after the first word, if it is short
\- before the last word
Otherwise space is inserted.
Should produce the same oubput as BibTeX.

```pycon
>>> print(join(['a', 'long', 'long', 'road']))
a~long long~road
>>> print(join(['very', 'long', 'phrase']))
very long~phrase
```

classes.name: pybtex.bibtex.names.NameFormat

methods.name: pybtex.bibtex.names.NameFormat.to_python
documentation: Convert BibTeX name format to Python (inexactly).

BibTeX name format string.

```pycon
>>> f = NameFormat('{ff~}{vv~}{ll}{, jj}')
>>> f.parts == [
...     NamePart(['', 'ff', None, '']),
...     NamePart(['', 'vv', None, '']),
...     NamePart(['', 'll', None, '']),
...     NamePart([', ', 'jj', None, ''])
... ]
True
>>> f = NameFormat('{{ }ff~{ }}{vv~{- Test text here -}~}{ll}{, jj}')
>>> f.parts == [
...     NamePart(['{ }', 'ff', None, '~{ }']),
...     NamePart(['', 'vv', None, '~{- Test text here -}']),
...     NamePart(['', 'll', None, '']),
...     NamePart([u', ', 'jj', None, ''])
... ]
True
>>> f = NameFormat('abc def {f~} xyz {f}?')
>>> f.parts == [
...     Text('abc def '),
...     NamePart(['', 'f', None, '']),
...     Text(' xyz '),
...     NamePart(['', 'f', None, '']),
...     Text('?'),
... ]
True
>>> f = NameFormat('{{abc}{def}ff~{xyz}{#@$}}')
>>> f.parts == [NamePart(['{abc}{def}', 'ff', None, '~{xyz}{#@$}'])]
True
>>> f = NameFormat('{{abc}{def}ff{xyz}{#@${}{sdf}}}')
>>> f.parts == [NamePart(['{abc}{def}', 'ff', 'xyz', '{#@${}{sdf}}'])]
True
>>> f = NameFormat('{f.~}')
>>> f.parts == [NamePart(['', 'f', None, '.'])]
True
>>> f = NameFormat('{f~.}')
>>> f.parts == [NamePart(['', 'f', None, '~.'])]
True
>>> f = NameFormat('{f{.}~}')
>>> f.parts == [NamePart(['', 'f', '.', ''])]
True
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.wrap
documentation:
Wrap long string into multiple lines by inserting line breaks.

The string is broken at whitespace characters so that each line is as long
as possible, but no longer than `width` characters.

If there are no possible break points in the first `width` characters, a
longer line will be produced, with the line break inserted at the first
possible whitespace characters after `width`.

After each line break, the subsequent line is indented with
`subsequent_indent` (two spaces by default).

The lines are not allowed to be shorter than `len(subsequent_indent) + 1`
(3 characters by default), so that each line contains at least one
non-whitespace character after the indent.

```pycon
>>> print(wrap('', width=3))
<BLANKLINE>
>>> print(wrap('0123456789 12345', width=10))
0123456789
  12345
>>> print(wrap('01234 6789 12345', width=10))
01234 6789
  12345
>>> print(wrap('01234 6789 12345', width=11))
01234 6789
  12345
>>> print(wrap('01234 6789 12345', width=9))
01234
  6789
  12345
>>> print(wrap(' a b c', width=3))
 a b
  c
>>> print(wrap('aa bb c', width=3))
aa bb
  c
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.change_case
documentation:
```pycon
>>> print(change_case('aBcD', 'l'))
abcd
>>> print(change_case('aBcD', 'u'))
ABCD
>>> print(change_case('ABcD', 't'))
Abcd
>>> print(change_case(r'The {\TeX book \noop}', 'u'))
THE {\TeX BOOK \noop}
>>> print(change_case(r'And Now: BOOO!!!', 't'))
And now: Booo!!!
>>> print(change_case(r'And {Now: BOOO!!!}', 't'))
And {Now: BOOO!!!}
>>> print(change_case(r'And {Now: {BOOO}!!!}', 'l'))
and {Now: {BOOO}!!!}
>>> print(change_case(r'And {\Now: BOOO!!!}', 't'))
And {\Now: booo!!!}
>>> print(change_case(r'And {\Now: {BOOO}!!!}', 'l'))
and {\Now: {booo}!!!}
>>> print(change_case(r'{\TeX\ and databases\Dash\TeX DBI}', 't'))
{\TeX\ and databases\Dash\TeX DBI}
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.bibtex_substring
documentation:
Return a substring of the given length, starting from the given position.

start and length are 1-based. If start is \< 0, it is counted from the end
of the string. If start is 0, an empty string is returned.

```pycon
>>> print(bibtex_substring('abcdef', 1, 3))
abc
>>> print(bibtex_substring('abcdef', 2, 3))
bcd
>>> print(bibtex_substring('abcdef', 2, 1000))
bcdef
>>> print(bibtex_substring('abcdef', 0, 1000))
<BLANKLINE>
>>> print(bibtex_substring('abcdef', -1, 1))
f
>>> print(bibtex_substring('abcdef', -1, 2))
ef
>>> print(bibtex_substring('abcdef', -2, 3))
cde
>>> print(bibtex_substring('abcdef', -2, 1000))
abcde
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.bibtex_len
documentation:
Return the number of characters in the string.

Braces are ignored. "Special characters" are ignored. A "special character"
is a substring at brace level 1, if the first character after the opening
brace is a backslash, like in "de la Vall{'e}e Poussin".

```pycon
>>> print(bibtex_len(r"de la Vall{\'e}e Poussin"))
20
>>> print(bibtex_len(r"de la Vall{e}e Poussin"))
20
>>> print(bibtex_len(r"de la Vallee Poussin"))
20
>>> print(bibtex_len(r'\ABC 123'))
8
>>> print(bibtex_len(r'{\abc}'))
1
>>> print(bibtex_len(r'{\abc'))
1
>>> print(bibtex_len(r'}\abc'))
4
>>> print(bibtex_len(r'\abc}'))
4
>>> print(bibtex_len(r'\abc{'))
4
>>> print(bibtex_len(r'level 0 {1 {2}}'))
11
>>> print(bibtex_len(r'level 0 {\1 {2}}'))
9
>>> print(bibtex_len(r'level 0 {1 {\2}}'))
12
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.bibtex_width
documentation:
Determine the width of the given string, in relative units.

```pycon
>>> bibtex_width('')
0
>>> bibtex_width('abc')
1500
>>> bibtex_width('ab{c}')
2500
>>> bibtex_width(r"ab{\'c}")
1500
>>> bibtex_width(r"ab{\'c{}}")
1500
>>> bibtex_width(r"ab{\'c{}")
1500
>>> bibtex_width(r"ab{\'c{d}}")
2056
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.bibtex_prefix
documentation:
Return the firxt num_char characters of the string.

Braces and "special characters" are ignored, as in bibtex_len.  If the
resulting prefix ends at brace level > 0, missing closing braces are
appended.

```pycon
>>> print(bibtex_prefix('abc', 1))
a
>>> print(bibtex_prefix('abc', 5))
abc
>>> print(bibtex_prefix('ab{c}d', 3))
ab{c}
>>> print(bibtex_prefix('ab{cd}', 3))
ab{c}
>>> print(bibtex_prefix('ab{cd', 3))
ab{c}
>>> print(bibtex_prefix(r'ab{\cd}', 3))
ab{\cd}
>>> print(bibtex_prefix(r'ab{\cd', 3))
ab{\cd}
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.bibtex_purify
documentation:
Strip special characters from the string.

```pycon
>>> print(bibtex_purify('Abc 1234'))
Abc 1234
>>> print(bibtex_purify('Abc  1234'))
Abc  1234
>>> print(bibtex_purify('Abc-Def'))
Abc Def
>>> print(bibtex_purify('Abc-~-Def'))
Abc   Def
>>> print(bibtex_purify('{XXX YYY}'))
XXX YYY
>>> print(bibtex_purify('{XXX {YYY}}'))
XXX YYY
>>> print(bibtex_purify(r'XXX {\YYY} XXX'))
XXX  XXX
>>> print(bibtex_purify(r'{XXX {\YYY} XXX}'))
XXX YYY XXX
>>> print(bibtex_purify(r'\\abc def'))
abc def
>>> print(bibtex_purify('a@#$@#$b@#$@#$c'))
abc
>>> print(bibtex_purify(r'{\noopsort{1973b}}1973'))
1973b1973
>>> print(bibtex_purify(r'{sort{1973b}}1973'))
sort1973b1973
>>> print(bibtex_purify(r'{sort{\abc1973b}}1973'))
sortabc1973b1973
>>> print(bibtex_purify(r'{\noopsort{1973a}}{\switchargs{--90}{1968}}'))
1973a901968
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.scan_bibtex_string
documentation:
Yield (char, brace_level) tuples.

"Special characters", as in bibtex_len, are treated as a single character

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.split_name_list
documentation:
Split a list of names, separated by ' and '.

```pycon
>>> split_name_list('Johnson and Peterson')
[u'Johnson', u'Peterson']
>>> split_name_list('Johnson AND Peterson')
[u'Johnson', u'Peterson']
>>> split_name_list('Johnson AnD Peterson')
[u'Johnson', u'Peterson']
>>> split_name_list('Armand and Peterson')
[u'Armand', u'Peterson']
>>> split_name_list('Armand and anderssen')
[u'Armand', u'anderssen']
>>> split_name_list('{Armand and Anderssen}')
[u'{Armand and Anderssen}']
>>> split_name_list('What a Strange{ }and Bizzare Name! and Peterson')
[u'What a Strange{ }and Bizzare Name!', u'Peterson']
>>> split_name_list('What a Strange and{ }Bizzare Name! and Peterson')
[u'What a Strange and{ }Bizzare Name!', u'Peterson']
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils._find_closing_brace
documentation:
```pycon
>>> _find_closing_brace('')
(u'', u'')
>>> _find_closing_brace('no braces')
(u'no braces', u'')
>>> _find_closing_brace('brace at the end}')
(u'brace at the end}', u'')
>>> _find_closing_brace('two closing braces}}')
(u'two closing braces}', u'}')
>>> _find_closing_brace('two closing} braces} and some text')
(u'two closing}', u' braces} and some text')
>>> _find_closing_brace('more {nested{}}{braces}} and the rest}')
(u'more {nested{}}{braces}}', u' and the rest}')
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.split_tex_string
documentation:
Split a string using the given separator (regexp).

Everything at brace level > 0 is ignored.

```pycon
>>> split_tex_string('')
[]
>>> split_tex_string('     ')
[]
>>> split_tex_string('.a.b.c.', r'\.')
[u'', u'a', u'b', u'c', u'']
>>> split_tex_string('.a.b.c.{d.}.', r'\.')
[u'', u'a', u'b', u'c', u'{d.}', u'']
>>> split_tex_string('Matsui      Fuuka')
[u'Matsui', u'Fuuka']
>>> split_tex_string('{Matsui      Fuuka}')
[u'{Matsui      Fuuka}']
>>> split_tex_string(r'Matsui\ Fuuka')
[u'Matsui', u'Fuuka']
>>> split_tex_string(r'{Matsui\ Fuuka}')
[u'{Matsui\\ Fuuka}']
>>> split_tex_string('a')
[u'a']
>>> split_tex_string('on a')
[u'on', u'a']
>>> split_tex_string(r'Qui\~{n}onero-Candela, J.')
[u'Qui\\~{n}onero-Candela,', u'J.']
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.bibtex_first_letter
documentation:
Return the first letter or special character of the string.

```pycon
>>> print(bibtex_first_letter('Andrew Blake'))
A
>>> print(bibtex_first_letter('{Andrew} Blake'))
A
>>> print(bibtex_first_letter('1Andrew'))
A
>>> print(bibtex_first_letter(r'{\TeX} markup'))
{\TeX}
>>> print(bibtex_first_letter(''))
<BLANKLINE>
>>> print(bibtex_first_letter('123 123 123 {}'))
<BLANKLINE>
>>> print(bibtex_first_letter(r'\LaTeX Project Team'))
L
```

functions.name: pybtex.bibtex.utils.bibtex_abbreviate
documentation:
Abbreviate string.

```pycon
>>> print(bibtex_abbreviate('Andrew Blake'))
A
>>> print(bibtex_abbreviate('Jean-Pierre'))
J.-P
>>> print(bibtex_abbreviate('Jean--Pierre'))
J.-P
```

modules.name: pybtex.richtext
documentation:
(simple but) rich text formatting tools

Usage:

```pycon
>>> t = Text('this ', 'is a ', Tag('em', 'very'), Text(' rich', ' text'))
>>> print(t.render_as('latex'))
this is a \emph{very} rich text
>>> print(six.text_type(t))
this is a very rich text
>>> t = t.capitalize().add_period()
>>> print(t.render_as('latex'))
This is a \emph{very} rich text.
>>> print(six.text_type(t))
This is a very rich text.
>>> print(Symbol('ndash').render_as('latex'))
--
>>> t = Text('Some ', Tag('em', Text('nested ', Tag('tt', 'Text', Text(' objects')))), '.')
>>> print(t.render_as('latex'))
Some \emph{nested \texttt{Text objects}}.
>>> print(six.text_type(t))
Some nested Text objects.
>>> t = t.upper()
>>> print(t.render_as('latex'))
SOME \emph{NESTED \texttt{TEXT OBJECTS}}.
>>> print(six.text_type(t))
SOME NESTED TEXT OBJECTS.
```

```pycon
>>> t = Text(', ').join(['one', 'two', Tag('em', 'three')])
>>> print(t.render_as('latex'))
one, two, \emph{three}
>>> print(six.text_type(t))
one, two, three
>>> t = Text(Symbol('nbsp')).join(['one', 'two', Tag('em', 'three')])
>>> print(t.render_as('latex'))
one~two~\emph{three}
>>> print(six.text_type(t))
one<nbsp>two<nbsp>three
```

functions.name: pybtex.richtext.str_repr
documentation:
```pycon
>>> print(str_repr('test'))
'test'
>>> print(str_repr(u'test'))
'test'
```
workaround for doctests in Python 2/3

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText.__add__
documentation:
Concatenate this Text with another Text or string.

```pycon
>>> Text('Longcat is ') + Tag('em', 'long')
Text('Longcat is ', Tag('em', 'long'))
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText.append
documentation: Append text to the end of this text. Normally, this is the same as concatenating texts with +, but for tags and similar objects the appended text is placed \_inside\_ the tag. ```pycon >>> text = Tag('em', 'Look here') >>> print((text + '!').render_as('html')) Look here! >>> print(text.append('!').render_as('html')) Look here! ```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText.join
documentation:
Join a list using this text (like string.join)

```pycon
>>> letters = ['a', 'b', 'c']
>>> print(six.text_type(String('-').join(letters)))
a-b-c
>>> print(six.text_type(String('-').join(iter(letters))))
a-b-c
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText.startswith
documentation:
Return True if string starts with the prefix,
otherwise return False.

prefix can also be a tuple of suffixes to look for.

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText.endswith
documentation:
Return True if the string ends with the specified suffix,
otherwise return False.

suffix can also be a tuple of suffixes to look for.

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText.add_period
documentation:
Add a period to the end of text, if the last character is not ".", "!" or "?".

```pycon
>>> text = Text("That's all, folks")
>>> print(six.text_type(text.add_period()))
That's all, folks.
```

```pycon
>>> text = Text("That's all, folks!")
>>> print(six.text_type(text.add_period()))
That's all, folks!
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText.capfirst
documentation:
Capitalize the first letter of the text.

```pycon
>>> Text(Tag('em', 'long Cat')).capfirst()
Text(Tag('em', 'Long Cat'))
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText.capitalize
documentation:
Capitalize the first letter of the text and lowercase the rest.

```pycon
>>> Text(Tag('em', 'LONG CAT')).capitalize()
Text(Tag('em', 'Long cat'))
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText.render_as
documentation: Render this {py:class}`Text` into markup. This is a wrapper method that loads a formatting backend plugin and calls {py:meth}`Text.render`. ```pycon >>> text = Text('Longcat is ', Tag('em', 'looooooong'), '!') >>> print(text.render_as('html')) Longcat is looooooong! >>> print(text.render_as('latex')) Longcat is \emph{looooooong}! >>> print(text.render_as('text')) Longcat is looooooong! ``` ``` param backend_name The name of the output backend (like "latex" or "html"). ```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText._unpack
documentation:
For Text object, iterate over all text parts.
Else, yield the object itself.

Used for unpacking Text objects passed as children to another Text object.

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseText._typeinfo
documentation: Return the type of this object and its parameters (not including the actual text content). Used for: - merging similar tags together (AB -> AB), - creating similar text objects with different text content.

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.__init__
documentation:
Create a text object consisting of one or more parts.

Empty parts are ignored:

```pycon
>>> Text() == Text('') == Text('', '', '')
True
>>> Text('Word', '') == Text('Word')
True
```

Text() objects are unpacked and their children are included directly:

```pycon
>>> Text(Text('Multi', ' '), Tag('em', 'part'), Text(' ', Text('text!')))
Text('Multi ', Tag('em', 'part'), ' text!')
>>> Tag('strong', Text('Multi', ' '), Tag('em', 'part'), Text(' ', 'text!'))
Tag('strong', 'Multi ', Tag('em', 'part'), ' text!')
```

Similar objects are merged together:

```pycon
>>> Text('Multi', Tag('em', 'part'), Text(Tag('em', ' ', 'text!')))
Text('Multi', Tag('em', 'part text!'))
>>> Text('Please ', HRef('/', 'click'), HRef('/', ' here'), '.')
Text('Please ', HRef('/', 'click here'), '.')
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.__eq__
documentation:
Rich text objects support equality comparison:

```pycon
>>> Text('Cat') == Text('cat')
False
>>> Text('Cat') == Text('Cat')
True
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.__len__
documentation:
`len(text)` returns the number of characters in the text, ignoring
the markup:

```pycon
>>> len(Text('Long cat'))
8
>>> len(Text(Tag('em', 'Long'), ' cat'))
8
>>> len(Text(HRef('http://example.com/', 'Long'), ' cat'))
8
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.__contains__
documentation:
`value in text` returns `True` if any part of the `text`
contains the substring `value`:

```pycon
>>> 'Long cat' in Text('Long cat!')
True
```

Substrings splitted across multiple text parts are not matched:

```pycon
>>> 'Long cat' in Text(Tag('em', 'Long'), 'cat!')
False
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.__getitem__
documentation:
Slicing and extracting characters works like with regular strings,
formatting is preserved.

```pycon
>>> Text('Longcat is ', Tag('em', 'looooooong!'))[:15]
Text('Longcat is ', Tag('em', 'looo'))
>>> Text('Longcat is ', Tag('em', 'looooooong!'))[-1]
Text(Tag('em', '!'))
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText._slice_beginning
documentation:
Return a text consistng of the first slice_length characters
of this text (with formatting preserved).

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText._slice_end
documentation:
Return a text consistng of the last slice_length characters
of this text (with formatting preserved).

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.append
documentation: Append text to the end of this text. For Tags, HRefs, etc. the appended text is placed *inside* the tag. ```pycon >>> text = Tag('strong', 'Chuck Norris') >>> print((text + ' wins!').render_as('html')) Chuck Norris wins! >>> print(text.append(' wins!').render_as('html')) Chuck Norris wins! ```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.split
documentation:
```pycon
>>> Text('a + b').split()
[Text('a'), Text('+'), Text('b')]
```

```pycon
>>> Text('a, b').split(', ')
[Text('a'), Text('b')]
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.startswith
documentation:
Return True if the text starts with the given prefix.

```pycon
>>> Text('Longcat!').startswith('Longcat')
True
```

Prefixes split across multiple parts are not matched:

```pycon
>>> Text(Tag('em', 'Long'), 'cat!').startswith('Longcat')
False
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.endswith
documentation:
Return True if the text ends with the given suffix.

```pycon
>>> Text('Longcat!').endswith('cat!')
True
```

Suffixes split across multiple parts are not matched:

```pycon
>>> Text('Long', Tag('em', 'cat'), '!').endswith('cat!')
False
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.isalpha
documentation:
Return True if all characters in the string are alphabetic and there is
at least one character, False otherwise.

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.lower
documentation:
Convert rich text to lowercase.

```pycon
>>> Text(Tag('em', 'Long cat')).lower()
Text(Tag('em', 'long cat'))
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.upper
documentation:
Convert rich text to uppsercase.

```pycon
>>> Text(Tag('em', 'Long cat')).upper()
Text(Tag('em', 'LONG CAT'))
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText.render
documentation:
Render this {py:class}`Text` into markup.

```
param backend

The formatting backend (an instance of
).
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText._typeinfo
documentation:
Return the type and the parameters used to create this text object.

```pycon
>>> text = Tag('strong', 'Heavy rain!')
>>> text._typeinfo() == (Tag, ('strong',))
True
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText._create_similar
documentation:
Create a new text object of the same type with the same parameters,
with different text content.

```pycon
>>> text = Tag('strong', 'Bananas!')
>>> text._create_similar(['Apples!'])
Tag('strong', 'Apples!')
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.BaseMultipartText._merge_similar
documentation:
Merge adjacent text objects with the same type and parameters together.

```pycon
>>> text = Text()
>>> parts = [Tag('em', 'Breaking'), Tag('em', ' '), Tag('em', 'news!')]
>>> list(text._merge_similar(parts))
[Tag('em', 'Breaking news!')]
```

classes.name: pybtex.richtext.String

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.String.__init__
documentation:
All arguments must be plain unicode strings.
Arguments are concatenated together.

```pycon
>>> print(six.text_type(String('November', ', ', 'December', '.')))
November, December.
```

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.String.__eq__
documentation: Compare two {py:class}`.String` objects.

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.String.startswith
documentation:
Return True if string starts with the prefix,
otherwise return False.

prefix can also be a tuple of suffixes to look for.

methods.name: pybtex.richtext.String.endswith
documentation:
Return True if the string ends with the specified suffix,
otherwise return False.

suffix can also be a tuple of suffixes to look for.
return self.value.endswith(text)

A {py:class}`String` is a wrapper for a plain Python string.

```pycon
>>> from pybtex.richtext import String
>>> print(String('Crime & Punishment').render_as('text'))
Crime & Punishment
>>> print(String('Crime & Punishment').render_as('html'))
Crime & Punishment
```

{py:class}`String` supports the same methods as {py:class}`Text`.

classes.name: pybtex.richtext.Text
documentation:
The {py:class}`Text` class is the top level container that may contain
{py:class}`String`, {py:class}`Tag` or {py:class}`HRef` objects.

classes.name: pybtex.richtext.Tag
documentation: A {py:class}`Tag` represents something like an HTML tag or a LaTeX formatting command: ```pycon >>> from pybtex.richtext import Tag >>> tag = Tag('em', 'The TeXbook') >>> print(tag.render_as('html')) The TeXbook >>> print(tag.render_as('latex')) \emph{The TeXbook} ``` {py:class}`Tag` supports the same methods as {py:class}`Text`.

classes.name: pybtex.richtext.HRef
documentation: A {py:class}`HRef` represends a hyperlink: ```pycon >>> from pybtex.richtext import Tag >>> href = HRef('http://ctan.org/', 'CTAN') >>> print(href.render_as('html')) CTAN >>> print(href.render_as('latex')) \href{http://ctan.org/}{CTAN} ``` ```pycon >>> href = HRef(String('http://ctan.org/'), String('http://ctan.org/')) >>> print(href.render_as('latex')) \url{http://ctan.org/} ``` {py:class}`HRef` supports the same methods as {py:class}`Text`.

classes.name: pybtex.richtext.Protected
documentation: A {py:class}`Protected` represents a "protected" piece of text. - {py:meth}`Protected.lower`, {py:meth}`Protected.upper`, {py:meth}`Protected.capitalize`, and {py:meth}`Protected.capitalize()` are no-ops and just return the {py:class}`Protected` object itself. - {py:meth}`Protected.split` never splits the text. It always returns a one-element list containing the {py:class}`Protected` object itself. - In LaTeX output, {py:class}`Protected` is {surrounded by braces}. HTML and plain text backends just output the text as-is. ```pycon >>> from pybtex.richtext import Protected >>> text = Protected('The CTAN archive') >>> text.lower() Protected('The CTAN archive') >>> text.split() [Protected('The CTAN archive')] >>> print(text.render_as('latex')) {The CTAN archive} >>> print(text.render_as('html')) The CTAN archive ``` :::{versionadded} 0.20 :::

classes.name: pybtex.richtext.Symbol
documentation:
A special symbol. This class is rarely used and may be removed in
future versions.

Examples of special symbols are non-breaking spaces and dashes.

{py:class}`Symbol` supports the same methods as {py:class}`Text`.
